fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that auto-loading library thing we talked about last sprint. Basically the idea is that developers shouldn't have to write a bunch of require statements by hand — the system should just figure out what file maps to what constant name based on where the file lives. Like if you have a file called `users_controller.rb` it should just know that maps to `UsersController` without anyone telling it explicitly.

We also need it to handle the nested folder situation — same pattern we used for the namespace resolution in that old config module, you know the one. Directories should act as namespaces automatically.

There's also the reload story — in development people want to be able to change a file and have it pick up the new version without restarting. But we should make sure that only works if someone explicitly opts into it, otherwise it should blow up with a clear error.

Oh and the gem versioning edge case — someone brought this up in the thread — when it's the top-level version file directly under the package namespace it should behave differently than a nested one. Ask around if you're not sure what I mean by that.

Ignored paths, eager loading, duplicate root handling — all the usual stuff. Make sure stale constants get cleaned up properly on unload. Error messages need to be consistent and machine-readable.

One quick follow-up from the questions that came in: the default name conversion is still the simple basename rule, just a little more explicit. Each underscore-separated segment of the basename is capitalized and the segments are concatenated, so `users_controller` becomes `UsersController` and `point_3d_value` becomes `Point3dValue`. Numeric characters stay in the segment they belong to, with no special handling, and acronyms are not guessed unless there’s an explicit override registered. We do support an overrides map of basename strings to constant name strings, and if a basename is in that map we use that mapping verbatim instead of the default capitalization logic. Anything not in the map keeps using the normal conversion, and the override has to be declared before setup/scanning.

A couple more specifics on load/unload behavior too: unloading removes every constant that was loaded from a managed file and also every namespace module that was autovivified by the loader. After an unload those constants are no longer defined on their parent modules, and the next access should lazy load again from scratch. On the require side, a managed file required through the loader returns a truthy loaded result the first time, and a second require of the same path returns a falsy result to show it was already loaded. Same idea if the constant was already autoloaded by constant access first — a later require of that same relative path should still report it as already loaded.

On the error side, if a managed source file gets loaded but does not define the constant its path implies, we should report a normalized `expected_constant_missing` error with the name of the expected constant. That’s a loader contract failure, not a Ruby NameError. And if some path segment turns into something that is not a valid constant name, we should catch that during setup/scanning, not later on access. The example here is `foo-bar.rb`, which would infer `Foo-bar`; that should report a normalized `invalid_constant_name` error including the inferred name and whether the bad entry was a file or directory.

Also, roots need to be real existing directories, and internally we store them as absolute paths. It should be possible to query the aggregate list of all managed roots across multiple loader instances, and both the root count and the absolute path normalization should be visible through the public API.

And just to pin down the version-file thing more clearly: for a packaged library, the version file sitting directly one level below the package namespace directory maps to the all-caps constant `VERSION`. If the version file is nested deeper than that direct child level, it uses the normal basename mapping and becomes `Version`. Everything else that’s just a normal file keeps using the normal mapping regardless of its name. The namespace behavior is the same one we discussed before where directories autovivify Module constants and can share a namespace across multiple roots, matching Feature 3 implicit namespace loading, and the version naming behavior above is the same Feature 1.3 package version name mapping rule.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: Loader.new(roots:, overrides: {}, ignore: [], exclude_from_eager_load: [], enable_reloading: false)

trigger_keywords: which files are ignored during scanning
what file types are skipped
hidden directories and backup files
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: During root scanning the loader ignores anything that is not a `.rb` source file. Specifically: files with non-`.rb` extensions (e.g. `.txt`, `.yaml`), backup files ending in `~`, editor artifact files whose name starts with `#`, and any directory whose name starts with `.` (hidden directories such as `.idea`) are all excluded and will never produce available constants.

trigger_keywords: how does namespace directory work
implicit namespace creation
directories as modules
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the loader encounters a directory whose name infers a constant that is not yet defined, it autovivifies a Module for that namespace on first access. Nested source files under that directory then become available as constants scoped under the new module. This works recursively through multiple directory levels. When multiple roots share a subdirectory name for the same namespace, the namespace is shared and nested constants from all matching roots remain available.

trigger_keywords: namespace file and directory both exist
explicit namespace with matching directory
source file and folder with same name
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If there is both a source file for a namespace (e.g. `hotel.rb`) and a matching subdirectory (e.g. `hotel/`), accessing the namespace constant loads the source file first. After that load completes, all files nested under the matching directory are still available as constants scoped inside that namespace. The behavior must not depend on a `name` override or any other user-visible attribute of the namespace object.

trigger_keywords: duplicate constant in multiple roots
same file in two roots
which root wins when there is a conflict
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When two or more configured roots each contain a source file that infers the same constant name, the file found through the root that was registered first takes precedence. All later duplicates for that same constant are silently shadowed and will never be loaded.

trigger_keywords: how to exclude specific files from loading
ignore configuration
glob patterns in ignore list
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You can supply a list of ignored paths that can be individual file paths, directory paths, or glob patterns. Anything matching an ignored path is excluded from both lazy loading and eager loading. Non-ignored siblings in the same directory remain available as normal. An ignored directory means no constants from that subtree will be registered at all.

trigger_keywords: eager load all files at once
how does eager loading work
load everything before access
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Calling eager load evaluates every reachable managed source file under all configured roots, including deeply nested files, before any explicit constant access. You can also supply an `exclude_from_eager_load` list of paths; those files are skipped during eager loading but remain available for normal lazy loading when the constant is accessed later.

trigger_keywords: reload behavior
how to reload changed files
pick up file changes without restart
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Reloading must be explicitly enabled before setup is called. When enabled, calling reload removes all constants that were loaded from managed files, including autovivified namespaces, and the next access re-evaluates the files from disk so updated content is picked up. If reload is called but reloading was not enabled before setup, the system raises a normalized `reloading_disabled` error.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: only works if someone explicitly opts into it, otherwise it should blow up with a clear error
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 8.2: reloading must be enabled via enable_reloading flag before setup; calling reload without it raises a normalized error=reloading_disabled, as specified in feature8_2_reloading_disabled_error.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: stale constants get cleaned up properly on unload
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 9.1 unload_removes_loaded_constants: both explicitly loaded constants and autovivified namespace modules are removed from their parent modules, as specified in feature9_1_unload_removes_loaded_constants.json

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can clarify off the top of my head — you'd want to dig into the existing test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ or look at how the PRD describes the relevant feature. The test JSON files are the ground truth for expected behavior.

feature10_1_require_idempotence.json.description: A managed file required through the normal loader path returns loaded once, and a second require reports that it was already loaded.

feature10_2_autoload_then_require_idempo.description: After a managed constant is autoloaded by constant access, requiring the same relative path reports that it is already loaded.

feature11_1_missing_expected_constant_er.description: If a managed source file does not define the constant implied by its path, report the expected constant as a normalized contract error.

feature11_2_invalid_inferred_constant_na.description: If a path segment maps to an invalid constant name, setup reports a normalized invalid-name error for that path entry.

feature12_1_root_directory_registration..description: Accept existing root directories as absolute managed roots and expose the aggregate list of roots across loaders.

feature1_1_default_name_mapping.json.description: Map underscore-separated file basenames to constant names using simple capitalization for each segment.

cases.expected_output:
basename=user
constant=User
basename=users_controller
constant=UsersController
basename=point_3d_value
constant=Point3dValue
basename=html_parser
constant=HtmlParser

feature1_2_custom_name_overrides.json.description: Apply explicit basename-to-constant-name mappings while preserving default mapping for basenames without an override.

cases.expected_output:
basename=html_parser
constant=HTMLParser
basename=csv_controller
constant=CSVController
basename=mysql_adapter
constant=MySQLAdapter
basename=users_controller
constant=UsersController

feature1_3_package_version_name_mapping..description: For a library root file, map the direct version file under the library namespace to an all-caps version constant and map other version files normally.

cases.expected_output:
path=main_version_file
constant=VERSION
path=nested_version_file
constant=Version
path=ordinary_file
constant=Widget

feature2_1_lazy_top_level_loading.json.description: Load top-level constants from matching source files on first access across one or more configured root directories.

feature2_2_non_code_entries_ignored.json.description: Ignore files that are not loadable source files and ignore hidden directories while scanning roots.

feature3_1_implicit_namespace_loading.js.description: Create missing namespace modules from directories and then load constants stored below those directories.

feature3_2_shared_namespace_across_roots.description: Combine matching subdirectories from multiple roots into one namespace so constants from each root remain available.

feature4_1_explicit_namespace_loading.js.description: When a namespace has its own source file and a matching directory, load the namespace file and keep its nested constants available.

cases.expected_output:
constant=Hotel
value=class
constant=Hotel::X
value=1
constant=Hotel::Pricing
value=class

feature5_1_first_root_precedence.json.description: When two roots contain files for the same top-level constant, load the first root file and leave later duplicates shadowed.

feature6_1_ignore_files_and_directories..description: Exclude configured files, directories, and glob matches from both lazy loading and eager loading.

cases.expected_output:
constant=X
available=yes
value=true
constant=Y
available=no
constant=M
available=no

feature7_1_eager_load_all_reachable_file.description: Load every reachable source file under configured roots before any explicit constant access.

cases.expected_output:
signal=app0_foo
value=loaded
signal=app1_baz
value=loaded
constant=App0::Foo
value=class
constant=App1::Foo::Bar::Baz
value=class

feature7_2_eager_load_exclusions_remain_.description: Paths excluded from eager loading are not evaluated during eager loading but still remain available for later lazy loading.

cases.expected_output:
signal=bar
value=unset
constant=Foo
value=true
constant=Bar
value=true

feature8_1_reload_refreshes_values.json.description: When reloading is enabled, a reload removes previously loaded objects and reads updated file contents on the next access.

cases.expected_output:
before=X
value=1
before=Y::A
value=1
before=Z::A
value=1
after=X
value=2
after=Y::A
value=2
after=Z::A
value=2

feature8_2_reloading_disabled_error.json.description: If reloading was not enabled before setup, requesting a reload reports a normalized reloading-disabled error.

feature9_1_unload_removes_loaded_constan.description: Unloading removes constants loaded from managed files and autovivified namespaces from their parents.
